sections.title: Let's Take a Look Around Us!
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with Khushi arriving at school and drawing her bright classroom. Students are invited to name the objects in her drawing and identify what materials they are made of. This introductory section sparks curiosity about the origin and composition of everyday things around us.

tasks.task_text: Draw a picture of your classroom in your notebook. Label the things that you have drawn.

Find as many things or parts of things, that are made of metals. Which metals do you recognise around you? If you do not know the name of the metal, ask your friends or an elder. Make a list of these metals in your notebook.
What are other things in the classroom, such as, mats, bulbs and electric switches, made of?

sections.title: See-through Materials!
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the concept of transparency through Khushi's experience of seeing a tree through a glass windowpane. Students learn that materials can be classified as transparent (you can see through clearly), translucent (partially see-through), or opaque (cannot see through at all). A practical activity has students test various objects against a light source to explore these categories.

tasks.task_text: Collect a few small objects of different materials from your surroundings like bottles, papers, cloth and utensils, etc. Look at a light bulb or a candle flame through them. You can see through some objects very clearly, you can partially see through some others, while you cannot see through some objects at all. Order these objects from those you can see through very clearly, to those you cannot see through at all.

Collect two or three see through bags, bottles or thin cloth of different colours. Look at a sheet of white paper through them. Does the colour of the paper appear to change? Does white paper appear different when you look at it through thin blue plastic or glass? Or, thin yellow plastic or glass? Do the colours of different objects appear to change? How did a blue object look through thin yellow plastic? Have you earlier experienced looking through coloured transparent objects? Try to recall such experiences.

sections.title: What is it made of?
sections.content_summary: Students explore the materials that common classroom objects are made from — wood, metal, glass, and plastic — and trace these materials back to their natural sources (e.g., wood comes from trees, metals from ore in the Earth, glass from sand). A 'Chain Game' writing activity asks students to fill a table grouping objects by material and add their own examples. Students also interview grandparents about changes in materials over time, connecting traditional and modern materials as part of Indian everyday culture.

tasks.task_text: In the table below, Khushi has grouped objects according to the materials that they are made of. The third column of the table is for you to complete. Here write the names of some objects you have seen that are made from that material. Some objects around you may be made from materials not in this list, e.g., clay and rubber are missing in Khushi's list. Use one of these to add an additional row in the table.
In their childhood, were these things made of the same materials?
Are there new materials now that they might not have seen before?
Are there any materials that they saw in their childhood that are not in use now? Why?

What material is your spoon made of? Is it made of metal, wood or some other material? Can you guess? Which of these words or phrases describes the spoon? Smooth / Rough / Dull / Shiny / Feels cold to the touch.

sections.title: Does it Bend?
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the properties of hardness and flexibility by asking whether fabric or rubber could replace wood in making a table. Students learn that wood is hard and rigid while fabric is soft and flexible, and that these physical properties determine suitability for different uses. A writing activity ('Odd Pairs') challenges students to pair objects with unsuitable materials and explain why those materials would not work.

tasks.task_text: Take a metal spoon and at least five objects made up of different materials—wood, metal, plastic, cloth and glass. Gently tap the spoon on each of them. Listen to the sound that each of them makes. Make your own words to describe all these different sounds. Try to capture those sounds in words, like ting-ting, dhum-dhum, dub-dub. Now compose your own beats.
List five objects and pair them with a material that is not suitable for it! Explain why these materials will not work to make these objects.

sections.title: Does it Flow?
sections.content_summary: Students learn that matter exists as solids, liquids, and gases, distinguished by whether they hold their shape. Solids (like stones, bricks, wood) do not change shape; liquids (like water) flow and take the shape of a container; gases (like air) do not stay in an open container. An activity asks students to sort given objects — ink, stone, smoke, ice, steam, spoon, honey, bottle, bag, water — into the three states and identify edge cases like sand, sponge, and clay.

tasks.task_text: Here are the names of some objects: ink, a stone, smoke, ice, steam, a spoon, honey, a bottle, a bag, water. If it is a solid, write its name on the tray; if it is a liquid, write its name in the bottle; if it is a gas, write its name in the balloon. Add some of your own objects in the tray, bottle and balloon. Some objects could be confusing, such as sand or a sponge or clay. Identify more such objects and write names of at least three of them.

sections.title: Natural — Artificial
sections.content_summary: The section distinguishes between natural things (occurring in nature — alive like plants and animals, or non-living like rocks, water, and air) and artificial things (made by people, like clothes, shoes, and tables). Khushi's drawing of a mango tree is used to illustrate natural things and to connect the concept to seasonal observation — when mangoes ripen. Students list five examples from each category.

tasks.task_text: List out five things in each group. Natural: _______  Artificial: _______

Have you seen trees around you that bear flowers and fruits at special times of the year? If you have ever eaten a ripe mango or seen mangoes in the market or watched a mango tree through the year, try to guess — at what time of the year did Khushi draw her picture? Could it be around January or around June?

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The closing reflection consolidates learning across all sections through four sub-tasks. Students write names of three common materials, discuss how to identify whether a shiny spoon is metal or painted, draw natural and artificial things, and match the terms transparent, translucent, and opaque to example objects.

tasks.task_text: Things around us are made of different types of materials. Write down the names of three materials we commonly see around us.
Suppose you find a shining spoon. You don't know if it is made of metal or whether it is made of some other material and then painted with shiny paint. How would you find out?
Draw three natural and three artificial things.
Match the pairs: Transparent — Translucent — Opaque with A lamp shade made of paper / A plate / A glass used in spectacles.

dual_strand.natural: Properties of materials — transparency, hardness, flexibility, and states of matter (solid, liquid, gas)
dual_strand.human_cultural: Human use of natural and artificial materials in everyday objects; intergenerational knowledge of changing materials in Indian households
